question_title: Will the interest in “tom steyer” change between 2026-04-16 and 2026-04-25 according to Google Trends?
summary:
Tom Steyer's Google Trends interest value on Apr 16 is 98 out of 100, nearly at the peak of the resolution date range (peak = 100 on Apr 13). This spike was driven by Eric Swalwell's exit from the CA governor race due to sexual assault allegations, catapulting Steyer to frontrunner status. 

For "Doesn't change," Apr 25 would need to be 95-100 — sustaining 95%+ of peak interest for 12 days past peak, which is extremely rare. For "Increases," Apr 25 would need to exceed 101 in the current scale (requiring a new, even larger peak that rescales everything). "Decreases" is the dominant outcome because virtually any decay from 98 exceeds the ±3 threshold.

A gubernatorial debate on April 22 is within the window and could create a boost, but Apr 25 (3 days post-debate) would still likely be well below the current starting value of 98. Monte Carlo simulations across multiple scenarios consistently yield 80-98% for Decreases.

factors.description: Apr 16 value is 98/100 - nearly at peak. The ±3 'Doesn't change' window covers only 95-101, an extremely narrow band. Almost any non-trivial decay resolves as Decreases.

The spike was driven by Swalwell exit (Apr 12-13) - a one-time shock event. Post-shock decay is the dominant dynamic over 9 days. Even active campaigns can't sustain peak-level search interest for 12+ days.

Gubernatorial debate scheduled for Apr 22 (Nexstar/NewsNation) - first debate featuring all leading candidates. Could generate a new spike, but Apr 25 is 3 days post-debate and post-debate interest typically decays quickly.

Active campaign heading toward June 2 primary (early voting May 4). Steyer is frontrunner with ongoing endorsements, policy announcements, and media coverage. This provides a higher floor than typical post-spike decay, but not enough to keep values at 95+.
A canceled debate (USC/KABC due to diversity criteria) introduces uncertainty about whether Apr 22 Nexstar debate happens. If canceled, no major catalyst to interrupt the decay.

reasoning:
**Forecast: Decreases (91%), Increases (5%), Doesn't change (4%)**

The key driver is threshold arithmetic: with Apr 16's value at 98 out of 100, the "Doesn't change" band is extremely narrow (95-101), and "Increases" requires exceeding the current peak. Almost any level of decay — even modest — exceeds the ±3 threshold and resolves as Decreases.

The Swalwell-exit shock that drove the spike is already 3 days old and will be nearly 2 weeks old by Apr 25. While a gubernatorial debate on April 22 could generate a secondary spike, Apr 25 is 3 days post-debate and unlikely to match the Apr 16 level of 98. Monte Carlo simulations across varying assumptions about debate impact and decay rates consistently put Decreases at 80-98%.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “tom steyer” change between 2026-04-16 and 2026-04-25 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43223)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=tom%20steyer&date=2026-03-26%202026-04-25](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=tom%20steyer&date=2026-03-26%202026-04-25) for 2026-04-25 is within 3 of the value at 2026-04-16. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "tom steyer",
  "date": "2026-03-26 2026-04-25",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
